_description: SwitchLingua 2.0 Stage 1 Layer 2: Group-level robust statistics for Hindi-English (Hinglish)
_note: Hinglish exhibits higher CMI than zh-en due to deep English integration in Indian education, media, and tech sectors. Based on SAIL, IIT Patna, and GLUECoS Hinglish corpus patterns.

SAIL_2017.reference: Jamatia et al. (2015); SAIL Shared Task on Code-Mixed Data

cmi_distribution._description: Overall CMI distribution [0,1], based on SAIL/IIT Hinglish corpus reports
cmi_distribution.source: Jamatia et al. (2015); Gamback & Das (2014) CMI definition; Bhat et al. (2018)

by_topic._note: Based on topic-CMI trends reported in Hinglish studies, not exact values

technology.note: Tech topics heavily use English vocabulary, CMI significantly higher

academic.note: Academic/education topics have high English term usage

daily_life.note: Daily topics lean Hindi but still moderate mixing

food.note: Food topics mostly Hindi, some English brand/cuisine names

entertainment.note: Bollywood/streaming discussions have notable English mixing

switch_type_distribution._description: Switch type distribution based on SAIL/IIT Hinglish corpus reports
switch_type_distribution.source: Jamatia et al. (2015); Poplack (1980) typology; Bhat et al. (2018)

intra_sentential.note: Intra-sentential switching dominates in Hinglish — Hindi grammar frame with English insertions is the norm

switch_position_distribution._description: Switch point syntactic positions — relatively robust across Hinglish corpora
switch_position_distribution.source: SAIL analysis; Bhat et al. (2018); Poplack Equivalence Constraint

NP_boundary.note: Noun phrase boundary most common — English nouns in Hindi syntax is the prototypical Hinglish pattern

VP_boundary.note: Verb phrase boundary — English verb stems with Hindi light verb (e.g., 'try करना', 'manage करना')

AdjP_AdvP.note: Adjective/adverb phrase — English adjectives very common in Hinglish

discourse_marker.note: Discourse markers (basically, actually, right, यार, मतलब)

high_frequency_L2_elements._description: High-frequency English elements in Hinglish (word frequency, noise-resistant)
high_frequency_L2_elements.source: SAIL/IIT Patna corpus reports + Hinglish common knowledge
L2_fixed_expressions_top30: at the end of the day

common_switch_triggers._note: Common context collocations that trigger switches

archetype_distribution_estimate._description: Estimated archetype proportions in real Hinglish data, based on CMI + switch type classification
archetype_distribution_estimate._method: Based on SAIL/IIT corpus CMI distribution and switch type patterns, speakers roughly mapped to nearest archetype

ARC_01_insertional.reasoning: CMI < 0.25 with primarily intra-sentential switching — common pattern of Hindi frame + English nouns

ARC_03_dense_mixer.reasoning: CMI > 0.45 — more prevalent in Hinglish than zh-en due to deeper English integration in Indian urban contexts

ARC_04_pragmatic.reasoning: CMI varies with pragmatic function — emphasis, humor, authority signaling

ARC_05_reluctant.reasoning: CMI < 0.10 — less common in urban Hinglish settings, but present in Hindi-dominant speakers

ARC_06_accommodation.reasoning: CMI varies significantly across interlocutors — common in cosmopolitan settings like Mumbai/Bangalore

validation_notes.confidence: Medium — based on published corpus statistics, not direct raw data computation
limitations: SAIL corpus is primarily social media text, which may skew towards higher CMI
IIT Patna data is relatively small (~6300 utterances)
Topic-CMI associations are qualitative trends, not precise values
Spoken Hinglish patterns may differ from text-based corpora
Regional variation (Delhi vs Mumbai vs Bangalore Hinglish) not fully captured
next_steps: Obtain SAIL raw annotations to compute precise aggregate statistics
Collect spoken Hinglish data to complement text-based corpus statistics
Validate archetype proportions against manually annotated speaker samples
Add regional sub-distributions for major Indian cities
